preamble: The shift from the voiced velar stop [g] to the voiceless velar fricative [x] involves two phonetic processes: spirantization (weakening from a stop to a fricative) and devoicing. This shift is a defining characteristic of West Germanic languages, particularly Dutch and dialects of German.

examples.note: Begedkefat: post-vocalic /g/ → [ɣ] (voiced velar fricative) in Biblical Hebrew. Note: atlas maps g→x as the closest equivalent; Semitic reflex is voiced [ɣ].
Word-final 'g' is consistently spirantized and devoiced in standard Dutch.
Initial 'g' is also spirantized, often devoicing in Northern dialects.
Colloquial and regional spirantization of final velar stops.

sources: Waterman: A History of the German Language
